title: Inelastic Neutron Scattering of the layered Kitaev ferromagnet Li$_3$Co$_2$SbO$_6$
authors: Abdul Basit, Richard A. Mole, Alex J. Brown, Jiatu Liu, Chris D. Ling, Stephan Rachel
abstract: Cobalt-based quantum magnets forming layered honeycomb arrangements have attracted much attention recently, as they are considered as a potential platform for materials with exotic Kitaev spin exchange. Amongst the discussed candidate materials are Na$_3$Co$_2$SbO$_6$ and Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$, both possessing a low-temperature ground state with magnetic zigzag ordering, similar to Na$_2$IrO$_3$ and $α$-RuCl$_3$. Here we report inelastic neutron scattering experiments on the quantum magnet Li$_3$Co$_2$SbO$_6$, which features ferromagnetic honeycomb planes with opposite magnetizations in neighboring planes. By comparing with linear spin wave theory, we show that the magnetic properties of Li$_3$Co$_2$SbO$_6$ can be well-modelled by an extended Kitaev--Heisenberg model, establishing it as a Kitaev-ferromagnet, or more specifically, as a Kitaev A-type antiferromagnet. Our analysis is complemented by magnetic field measurements and simulations.
summary: This paper identifies Li3Co2SbO6 as a Kitaev A-type antiferromagnet using inelastic neutron scattering. It demonstrates that the magnetic excitations and field-induced transitions are accurately described by an extended Kitaev-Heisenberg model. This provides a new material platform for studying Kitaev physics in a ferromagnetic-plane system.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the magnetic properties and exchange interactions of the layered honeycomb magnet Li3Co2SbO6 to determine if it acts as a Kitaev ferromagnet.
main_result: The researchers established Li3Co2SbO6 as a Kitaev A-type antiferromagnet, where magnetic properties are well-modeled by an extended Kitaev-Heisenberg model including significant Kitaev and off-diagonal Gamma interactions.
method: Inelastic Neutron Scattering (INS) was used for experimental measurements, while Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT) and numerical energy minimization were used for theoretical modeling and parameter fitting.
model_or_system: The system is Li3Co2SbO6, a layered cobalt-based quantum magnet with an A-type antiferromagnetic ground state. The model is an extended Kitaev-Heisenberg Hamiltonian including bond-dependent Kitaev (K), Heisenberg (J1), and off-diagonal (Gamma and Gamma') exchange terms.
key_observables: Magnetic Bragg reflections, dynamical structure factor (DSF), magnon excitation spectra, and magnetization curves.
important_parameters: Magnetic ordering temperature TN = 14 K; exchange parameters (J1, K, Gamma, Gamma', Jperp) = (-1.57, -5.90, 0.97, 0.35, 0.50) meV; magnetic field strengths up to 7 T.
main_assumptions: The magnetic unit cell is assumed to remain stable under an applied field, and the analysis uses a Holstein-Primakoff expansion truncated to leading order.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure; Fig 2 shows INS spectra at different wavelengths; Fig 3/4 shows the calculated DSF compared to experiment; Fig 5 shows neutron powder diffraction under magnetic fields; Fig 7 shows INS spectra at 7 T; Fig 8 shows classical spin configurations; Fig 10/11 show the evolution of spectra and gap opening with field orientation; Fig 12 checks for sample reorientation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and its importance, describes the crystal structure and experimental setup, presents zero-field INS and diffraction results, details the theoretical modeling using LSWT, investigates the effects of external magnetic fields on both diffraction and dynamics, and concludes with a comparison to related cobalt-based compounds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structure of the honeycomb phase of Li3Co2SbO6 viewed (a) perpendicular and (b) parallel to the honeycomb layers. CoO6 octahedra are blue, SbO6 octahe- dra are brown, Li atoms are green, and O atoms are red. Space group C2/m, 298 K lattice parameters a = 5.2717(6) ˚A, b = 9.0871(10) ˚A, c = 2.2039(6) ˚A, β = 110.1553(14)°.
FIG. 2. Powder-averaged INS spectra measured using neu- trons with wavelengths of (a) λ = 4.69 ˚A and (b) λ = 2.345 ˚A. The sample temperature for these measurements was set to 1.6 K.

FIG. 3. The DSF calculated for the best-fitted set of model parameters. The chosen parameter set is (J1, K, Γ, Γ′, J⊥) = (−1.57, −5.90, 0.97, 0.35, 0.50) meV. A Lorentzian broadening of γ = 0.45 meV was chosen phenomenologically.

FIG. 4. (a) Experimental INS spectrum used for fitting of the DSF. This is the same INS spectrum as shown in Fig. 2(b) but without the elastically broadened region. (b) Theoreti- cal, best-fitted powder-averaged DSF. The white stripes cor- respond to the constant-k cuts taken for (c) |k| = 0.7±0.1 ˚A −1
